title: Investigating corruption crimes under German law, articles and links

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: Investigating corruption crimes under German law, articles and links

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Corruption crimes in Germany fall primarily under the jurisdiction of the German Penal Code (Strafgesetzbuch - StGB). In addition to the provisions in the StGB, there are also other laws and regulations pertaining to corruption, such as the Law on Combating Corruption (Gesetz zur Bekämpfung der Korruption) and specific provisions in administrative and tax laws. Here are some articles and links relevant to investigating corruption crimes under German law:

1. German Penal Code (Strafgesetzbuch - StGB):
- The full text of the StGB can be found here (in German): https://www.gesetze-im-internet.de/stgb/index.html
- English translation of the German Penal Code: https://www.gesetze-im-internet.de/englisch_stgb/englisch_stgb.html
- Relevant provisions in the StGB include sections 331-335a, which deal with bribery and corruption offenses involving public officials, judges, and other civil servants

2. Law on Combating Corruption (Gesetz zur Bekämpfung der Korruption):
- Full text of the law in German: https://www.gesetze-im-internet.de/bdsg_1990/BJNR029550990.html
- This law contains provisions that aim to enhance the effectiveness of the fight against corruption, both in the public and private sectors.

3. The Federal Criminal Police Office (Bundeskriminalamt - BKA):
- Official website (in German): https://www.bka.de/
- The BKA is responsible for combating public corruption and coordinating investigations on a federal level.

4. The Federal Office of Justice (Bundesamt für Justiz - BfJ):
- Official website (in German): https://www.bundesjustizamt.de/
- The BfJ is responsible for monitoring criminal records, exchanging information on corruption offenses with other EU countries, and maintaining the German Register of Corruption.

5. Transparency International Germany (Transparency Deutschland):
- Official website (in German): https://www.transparency.de/
- As a German chapter of the global organization Transparency International, they work on raising public awareness about corruption issues and advocating for better anti-corruption policies in Germany.

6. OECD Anti-Bribery Convention:
- Germany is a party to the OECD Anti-Bribery Convention, which provides an international framework for combating foreign bribery.
- Overview of Germany's implementation of the convention: http://www.oecd.org/corruption/anti-bribery/germany-oecdanti-briberyconvention.htm

These resources should assist you in researching and understanding corruption crimes in Germany.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: Investigating corruption crimes in Great Britain

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
In the United Kingdom, which includes Great Britain, corruption crimes are primarily governed by the Bribery Act 2010. In addition to the Bribery Act, there are other laws, guidelines, and organizations involved in investigating and prosecuting corruption crimes. Below are some relevant articles and links related to investigating corruption crimes in the United Kingdom:

1. Bribery Act 2010:
- Full text of the Bribery Act 2010: http://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2010/23/contents
- This comprehensive legislation covers various aspects of bribery offenses, including offences relating to bribing another person, accepting a bribe and bribing foreign public officials.

2. Serious Fraud Office (SFO):
- Official website: https://www.sfo.gov.uk/
- The SFO is an independent government department responsible for investigating and prosecuting cases of serious or complex fraud, bribery, and corruption in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland.

3. Crown Prosecution Service (CPS):
- Official website: https://www.cps.gov.uk/
- The CPS is responsible for prosecuting criminal cases investigated by the police in England and Wales, which can include corruption cases.

4. National Crime Agency (NCA):
- Official website: https://www.nationalcrimeagency.gov.uk/
- The NCA operates as the UK's lead agency for combatting serious and organized crime, including corruption.

5. City of London Police Economic Crime Directorate:
- Official website: https://www.cityoflondon.police.uk/advice-and-support/fraud-and-economic-crime/Pages/default.aspx
- The City of London Police is the national policing lead for economic crime and is responsible for investigating financial crimes, including fraud and corruption.

6. UK Anti-Corruption Plan:
- The UK Anti-Corruption Plan sets out actions to combat corruption both domestically and internationally: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/uk-anti-corruption-plan

7. Transparency International UK:
- Official website: http://www.transparency.org.uk/
- As the UK chapter of the global organization Transparency International, they work on raising public awareness about corruption issues and advocating for better anti-corruption policies in the United Kingdom.

These resources will aid you in researching and understanding corruption crimes in the United Kingdom. Please note that this information is focused on England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Scotland has a separate legal system with different laws and institutions.
